---
title: Agritech Portal
date: 2025-06-10
slug: agritech-portal
---

Farmevo was a farm operations management platform designed for farmers and agricultural contractors. The system provided a unified portal where farm managers could plan and track field operations (spraying, harvesting, drilling, etc.), manage their fleet of vehicles and implements, assign jobs to operators and contractors, track field data across growing seasons, manage inventory and warehousing, and generate invoices. Contractors could log in to view assigned work, while operators received their job assignments with instructions, product requirements, and field locations displayed on an interactive satellite map. The platform supported role-based access for four distinct user types: Farmers, Contractors, Managers, and Operators, each with tailored views and permission boundaries enforced at both the API and database layers.

# Tech Stack

- Fastify \+ Mercurius (Backend API with GraphQL)
- React (Frontend SPA)
- Hasura GraphQL Engine (Primary data layer and real-time API)
- PostgreSQL (Database)
- URQL \+ Graphcache (Client-side GraphQL with normalized caching)
- Chakra UI (Component library)
- Google Maps API (Field polygon mapping and geospatial features)
- Firebase Hosting (Frontend deployment)
- AWS Elastic Beanstalk (API deployment)
- AWS ECS (Hasura \+ Postgres deployment)

# Key Challenges

**Role-Based Multi-Tenant Architecture**  
The platform served four user roles — Farmer, Contractor, Manager, and Operator — each with fundamentally different views of the same data.
![Signup](/case-studies/farmevo/signup.webp)

A Farmer could see all of their own fields, jobs, and team members.  
A Contractor could manage their own operations but also appear as an assignee on a Farmer's jobs.  
Operators had the most restricted view: they could only see jobs they were assigned to and were limited to updating extras and product values however, they couldn't modify job structure, fields, or status beyond requesting completion.

Solution:  
Hasura's role-based permission system was leveraged to enforce **row-level security** at the database layer. Three permission tiers (admin, user, operator) were configured with column-level and row-level filters. The user role could only access rows where user_id \= X-Hasura-User-Id, while the operator role was scoped to jobs where they existed in the job_operators junction table. The frontend used a GraphQL @skip(if: $isOperator) directive pattern to conditionally strip sensitive fields (billing rates, customer addresses, cost data) from queries based on the authenticated role, ensuring that even the network payload respected access boundaries. JWT claims were signed with RS256 keys shared between the Fastify API and Hasura, so both layers enforced identical identity guarantees.

**Interactive Geospatial Field Management**  
Farm fields were not simple text entries — they were geo-referenced polygons drawn on a satellite map. Users needed to create, edit, and version fields across growing seasons (e.g., a field might grow wheat in spring and be fallow in autumn). Each "season shot" was a snapshot of the field at a point in time, linked to a polygon with computed acreage. When creating a job, users selected fields by clicking polygons directly on the map, with real-time area calculations displayed.

Solution:  
PostgreSQL's native polygon and point types were used to store geometries directly in the database. On the frontend, the Google Maps JavaScript API rendered these polygons from stored coordinates using a custom fromDbPolygonToPathArray utility that parsed the Postgres polygon format ((lat,lng),(lat,lng),...) into google.maps.LatLng arrays. Area computation used google.maps.geometry.spherical.computeArea() converted to acres. Fields were versioned through a field_season_shot table with a unique constraint on (field_id, season), allowing the same physical field to have different crop data, ownership types, and notes per season — while maintaining a single polygon reference that could be independently updated.

**Complex Job Orchestration**

![Jovs](/case-studies/farmevo/ops.webp)

A single job involved up to eight related entities: an operation type, a contractor, multiple operators (each with assigned vehicles and implements), multiple field selections, product applications with lockable values, custom extras with chargeable units, and GPS pin-drop locations with instructions. Creating or editing a job required orchestrating inserts and upserts across seven junction tables (job_operators, job_operator_vehicles, job_operator_implements, job_fields, job_extras, job_products, job_locations) in a single mutation.

Solution:  
Hasura's nested insert with on_conflict upsert capabilities handled the creation path — a single insert_jobs_one mutation with nested relationship data created the entire job graph atomically. For edits, a custom generateUpdateJobMutation function dynamically generated the GraphQL mutation string at runtime. It computed the diff between the existing job state and the new form state, then produced targeted delete\_\* operations for removed associations and an insert_jobs_one with on_conflict for upserted data — all within one request. This avoided race conditions and ensured the job was never in a partially updated state. Operator edits were restricted to a separate UpdateJobAsOperator mutation that only touched job_extras and job_products values via update\_\*\_many batch operations.

**Client-Side Normalized Cache**  
With deeply nested, relational data flowing through GraphQL, maintaining cache consistency after mutations was critical. A user adding a job needed it to appear immediately in the jobs table. Editing a field polygon needed to reflect across the field list, snapshot history, and any jobs referencing that field.

Solution:  
URQL's @urql/exchange-graphcache was configured with a full normalized cache backed by the introspected Hasura schema. Custom keys were defined for every entity to handle composite primary keys (e.g., job_products keyed by jobId:productId, user_roles by userId:role). Resolver-level pagination was implemented via simplePagination for jobs, vehicles, and implements. Twelve custom UpdateResolver functions handled post-mutation cache writes — inspecting existing cache entries via cache.inspectFields and surgically updating query results without requiring a network refetch. Date fields on jobs (from, to, completedAt) were hydrated from ISO strings to Date objects at the resolver level, ensuring consistent date handling across the application.

**Multi-Step Onboarding Flow**  
New users needed to complete a four-step onboarding: email verification (OTP), business details, preferences (currency, timezone, default map location, field color), and tax/invoicing setup. This data spanned three database tables (users, user_profiles, user_prefs, user_tax_rates) and needed to be submitted atomically at the end.

Solution:

![][image3]

The onboarding was built as a stepped form using chakra-ui-steps, with form state accumulated in a parent component across all steps via parseFormData from the native FormData API. Each step rendered controlled inputs with defaultValue bindings, allowing users to navigate back and see their previously entered data. On final submission, a single SaveUserProfile mutation used Hasura's on_conflict upsert on all three tables simultaneously, ensuring atomicity. The email verification step used a server-generated 4-digit OTP (stored in a dedicated otps table with a ![][image4]5-minute TTL enforced by a WHERE now() \< expires_at clause) sent via Postmark email templates. The OTP verification was transactional — deleting the OTP and setting is_email_verified in a single transaction.

**Invoice Generation**  
Completed jobs needed printable invoices that calculated costs from operation billing rates, product prices, and billable area. The invoice pulled contractor and customer company details, addresses, and line-item breakdowns.

Solution:  
A JobInvoiceModal component rendered a fully formatted invoice within a ref-tracked DOM element, then used react-to-print to generate a downloadable PDF. Operation costs were editable (supporting both auto-calculated acre-based billing and manual entry) with an interactive input that locked on Enter. Product costs were computed as value × pricePerUnit and summed into subtotals. The invoice data was sourced entirely from the existing GraphQL cache, requiring no additional API calls.

**CI/CD & Deployment Pipeline**  
The monorepo used Yarn Workspaces with three packages (@farmevo/api, @farmevo/web, @farmevo/common). The common package was auto-compiled before either app started via prestart scripts. The API deployed to AWS Elastic Beanstalk via eb deploy, with staging/prod environments toggled through Makefile targets. The frontend deployed to Firebase Hosting with a staging-specific build via env-cmd. Hasura and Postgres ran as containerized services on AWS ECS, with metadata and migrations tracked locally through the Hasura CLI. GraphQL codegen (@graphql-codegen/client-preset) introspected the live Hasura schema and generated TypeScript types shared across the monorepo through the common package.

# Impact & Reflection

The Farmevo Portal demonstrated that a small team could build a feature-rich, enterprise-grade farm management system by leaning heavily on Hasura as the data layer — eliminating the need for hand-written CRUD endpoints and getting real-time subscriptions, role-based permissions, and a fully typed GraphQL API essentially for free.

- Type Safety End-to-End: From database columns through Hasura's auto-generated schema, through graphql-codegen into TypeScript types, into URQL's typed hooks — a column rename in a migration would surface as a compile error in the React component rendering it.
- Geospatial Capability Without GIS Overhead: By using PostgreSQL's native geometry types and Google Maps for rendering, the platform handled polygon-based field management without requiring PostGIS or a dedicated mapping backend.
- Role Isolation Without Code Duplication: The same React components served Farmers, Contractors, and Operators, with Hasura permissions ensuring data isolation and frontend conditional rendering adapting the UI — rather than maintaining separate applications per role.
